beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The plus-versus-minus combination explaining bonding versus antibonding through a midplane node reframed covalent bonds for me completely. Push it further. Please (1) show why the plus combination's constructive interference piles electron density between the protons and acts as Coulomb glue; (2) explain how the minus combination's midplane node removes the density that would counter proton-proton repulsion; (3) connect this to the quoted hydrogen-molecule-ion numbers, roughly 1.8 eV binding and 1.3 angstrom bond length, and tell me how the antibonding energy compares.

props.command: The plus-versus-minus combination explaining bonding versus antibonding through a midplane node reframed covalent bonds for me completely. Push it further. Please (1) show why the plus combination's constructive interference piles electron density between the protons and acts as Coulomb glue; (2) explain how the minus combination's midplane node removes the density that would counter proton-proton repulsion; (3) connect this to the quoted hydrogen-molecule-ion numbers, roughly 1.8 eV binding and 1.3 angstrom bond length, and tell me how the antibonding energy compares.

beats.narration_text: Two protons, one electron. Combine two atomic orbitals with a plus sign and the protons snap together into a molecule.
Combine the same two orbitals with a minus sign and the protons fly apart. Same atoms. Same electron. Different sign.
Both combinations feel the same two protons. One holds them together. One doesn't. What decides which way a combination binds?

props.headline: Both combinations feel the same two protons. One holds them together.…

card.headline: Same atoms. Different sign.
card.dek: One combination bonds. One doesn't. Why?

beats.narration_text: A classical picture says: the electron attracts both protons, so it should always pull them together. The sign of the combination shouldn't matter.
In quantum mechanics the electron is a wave. When two waves meet, they can add or cancel. Where the wave lands is where the electron tends to be.

props.headline: In quantum mechanics the electron is a wave. When two waves…

beats.narration_text: The plus combination adds the two orbital waves constructively. The result peaks exactly between the protons — the electron density piles up in the midplane.
That midplane density is attracted to both protons at once. It pulls them together like a Coulomb glue — that is a covalent bond.
The minus combination subtracts. The waves cancel at the midplane, leaving a node — zero electron density — exactly where the glue would have been.
Without density between them, there's nothing to counter the proton-proton repulsion. The antibonding orbital pushes the protons apart. The node is the reason.
This is the origin of every covalent bond in chemistry. Bonding means constructive interference between atomic orbitals — electron density concentrated between the nuclei.

Illustrative: in a hydrogen molecule ion, the bonding orbital gives a binding energy of about 1.8 electron-volts and a bond length near 1.3 angstroms. The antibonding orbital has no minimum — purely repulsive at all distances.
A covalent bond forms because adding orbitals builds density between the nuclei. The sign of the combination — not the atoms — decides whether a bond forms.

props.headline: A covalent bond forms because adding orbitals builds density between the…

card.headline: The sign decides the bond.
card.dek: Constructive interference builds density between nuclei. That density is the bond.

The antibonding orbital has no minimum — purely repulsive at all distances.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [the mechanism just described] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
